title: What is Vue.js and what are its core features?
description: Describe Vue and what makes it distinctive.
answer: **Vue.js** is a progressive JavaScript framework for building UIs. Core features: a reactive data system, a declarative template syntax, single-file components (`.vue`), a component-based architecture, and an approachable adoption curve — you can drop it into a page or build a full SPA.

title: What is a Single File Component (SFC)?
answer: An **SFC** is a `.vue` file bundling a component's `<template>` (markup), `<script>` (logic), and `<style>` (CSS, optionally `scoped`) in one file. This colocation keeps a component self-contained and supports tooling like scoped styles and hot reload.

title: How does Vue's reactivity system work?
answer: Vue 3 wraps state in a **Proxy** (`reactive`/`ref`) that intercepts reads to track dependencies and writes to trigger updates. When a reactive value used in a component's render changes, Vue re-runs that component's render function and patches the DOM. (Vue 2 used `Object.defineProperty`, which couldn't detect new properties — fixed by Proxies.)

title: What is the difference between ref and reactive?
answer: `ref()` wraps any value (primitive or object) and is accessed via `.value` in script (auto-unwrapped in templates). `reactive()` makes an **object** deeply reactive without `.value` but can't hold primitives and loses reactivity if destructured. Use `ref` for primitives/single values, `reactive` for objects you keep as a whole.

title: What is the difference between computed and watch?
description: When to use computed vs watchers.
answer: **computed** returns a cached, derived value that recomputes only when its dependencies change — use it for values derived from state. **watch**/`watchEffect` runs side effects in response to changes (API calls, logging). Rule of thumb: derive values with `computed`, perform actions with `watch`.

title: What is the Composition API and how does it differ from the Options API?
description: Compare the two component authoring styles.
answer: The **Options API** organizes a component by option type (`data`, `methods`, `computed`). The **Composition API** (`setup`/`<script setup>`) organizes by logical concern using `ref`, `computed`, and composables — improving reuse and TypeScript inference, especially in large components. Both can coexist.

title: What are Vue directives? Give common examples.
description: Explain directives like v-if, v-for, v-bind.
answer: **Directives** are special `v-` attributes that apply reactive behavior to the DOM: `v-if`/`v-show` (conditional render/display), `v-for` (lists), `v-bind` (`:`) (attributes), `v-on` (`@`) (events), and `v-model` (two-way binding on inputs).

title: What is the difference between v-if and v-show?
answer: `v-if` actually **adds/removes** the element from the DOM (and its component lifecycle) — better when the condition rarely changes. `v-show` keeps the element in the DOM and just toggles `display: none` — cheaper to toggle frequently but always rendered.

title: How do Vue components communicate?
answer: A parent passes data **down** via **props**; a child sends data **up** by **emitting events** (`$emit`/`defineEmits`). For two-way on a single value, `v-model` combines a prop and an event. Distant components share state via `provide/inject` or a store like Pinia.

title: How does v-model work on components?
answer: On a component `v-model` binds a prop (default `modelValue`) and listens for an `update:modelValue` event. The child receives `modelValue` and calls `emit('update:modelValue', newVal)` to update the parent. Vue 3 supports multiple named models like `v-model:title`.

title: What are Vue lifecycle hooks?
answer: Key hooks: `onBeforeMount`/`onMounted` (DOM ready), `onBeforeUpdate`/`onUpdated` (reactive change re-render), and `onBeforeUnmount`/`onUnmounted` (cleanup — remove listeners/timers). In the Composition API they're imported functions called inside `setup`.

title: What are slots in Vue?
description: Explain default, named, and scoped slots.
answer: **Slots** let a parent inject template content into a child. A **default slot** fills `<slot/>`; **named slots** (`<slot name="header"/>`) target multiple regions; **scoped slots** expose child data back to the parent's slot template, enabling flexible, reusable components like lists and tables.

title: What are composables in Vue?
answer: A **composable** is a function using the Composition API to encapsulate and reuse stateful logic (e.g. `useMouse()`, `useFetch()`). It returns reactive state and functions, replacing Vue 2 mixins with a cleaner, conflict-free, TypeScript-friendly pattern.

title: What is Pinia and why use it?
answer: **Pinia** is Vue's official state-management library (successor to Vuex). It offers typed stores with `state`, `getters`, and `actions`, a simpler API, great TypeScript support, and devtools integration — ideal for sharing state across many components.

title: What are provide and inject?
description: Explain dependency provision across the tree.
answer: **provide/inject** lets an ancestor supply data that any descendant can `inject`, avoiding prop-drilling through intermediate components. Provided reactive values stay reactive in consumers. It's commonly used by libraries and for app-wide config/themes.

title: Why is the :key attribute important in v-for?
answer: `:key` gives each list item a stable identity so Vue's virtual-DOM diff can match, reuse, and reorder elements correctly instead of patching in place. Without stable keys (e.g. using the index), you get subtle bugs with component state, inputs, and animations during reordering.

title: What is nextTick in Vue?
answer: Vue batches DOM updates asynchronously. **`nextTick()`** returns a promise that resolves after the DOM has been updated, so you can run code that needs the post-update DOM (e.g. measuring an element or focusing a newly rendered input).

title: What are scoped styles in Vue?
answer: `<style scoped>` limits CSS to the current component by adding a unique data attribute to its elements and to the selectors, so styles don't leak to other components. For deliberately styling child content you use `:deep()`.

title: What is Teleport in Vue 3?
answer: **`<Teleport to="...">`** renders its child content at a different place in the DOM (e.g. `body`) while keeping it logically inside the component. It's ideal for modals, tooltips, and dropdowns that must escape parent `overflow`/`z-index` constraints.

title: What is keep-alive in Vue?
answer: **`<KeepAlive>`** caches inactive component instances instead of destroying them, preserving their state (and DOM) when toggled or switched (e.g. tabs, routed views). It exposes `onActivated`/`onDeactivated` hooks and can be tuned with `include`/`exclude`/`max`.
